text: writings Liminality and the Modern 44 (van Gennep 1913b). In that period, van Gennep refused to see folklore and ethnography as radically separate disciplines. For him they were a single discipline with a shared methodology, dealing with what he always, and with emphasis, called ‘living facts’. In order to establish such a terrain, van Gennep found it necessary to decouple ethnology and ethnography from physical anthropology, and also from the study of history, or ‘cette manie orrible de subordonner l’étude de présent à celle du passé’ (van Gennep quoted in Zerilli, 1998a: 152). For van Gennep, true enough, habits and practices could not be derived from their historical ‘origin’, but had to be placed in their present reality. Van Gennep was certainly inspired by the positivist French social scientific literature. Like other writers of his generation, he saw definition, classification, and systematic comparison as crucial. He paid an almost manic attention to ethnographic facts. A new social science had to be both systematic and empirical. Exactly like Durkheim and Mauss, van Gennep lamented the fact that the social sciences still needed to build up a rigour and systematic approach that would give them the prestige and applicability of the natural sciences. Yet van Gennep was also different, and in some ways quite his own. He made one interesting remark about his own formative background which deserves attention: ‘I was lucky in regarding myself as a son of the French eighteenth century, not of the nineteenth’ (as in Belmont 1979: 133). In van Gennep’s reading, scientists of the nineteenth century had become overtly distanced from reality, and had started to adopt a language which had lost its directness to the world. Van Gennep was searching for a new science that could allow us to return to life. This is in fact what strikes one about van Gennep’s own writings: they are extremely forthright and ‘sincere’ (Belmont’s expression, ibid.), and yet comprehensive, always showing full awareness of the complexity and ‘relatedness’ of the discussed material. His whole approach was both extremely methodical, down to earth, and yet, based on his intuitive skills, linked to a larger picture – although it is true that the very nature of that ‘picture’ was not always fully drawn. Van Gennep was therefore quite sceptical about certain usages of scientific positivism, and he strongly criticized the Durkheimians on precisely these lines. Van Gennep was, in particular, highly critical of Durkheim’s comparative method, which, according to him, failed to compare like with like. He emphatically distanced himself from Durkheimian sociology, and instead proposed a social science methodology inspired by biology, ‘une biologie sociologique’, as he would call it. Already in 1909, in the second volume of Religions, moeurs et légendes, van Gennep argued that ‘when we wish to study social phenomena, we must study them both locally, with the help of the historical method, and also comparatively, with the aid of the biological method, so that we can classify them into “natural” categories: family, genus, species’ (here as translated by Hafstein 1999). In a later article from 1934, he located his own interest in biology to the age of thirteen, when his father (Paul) had him making microscope slides. As he said, ‘a Pasteurian training stays with one for life’ (in Belmont 1979: 15). The ‘biology’ to which van Gennep referred was therefore not simply an allusion to the authority Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 45 and objectivity of natural science, but most of all an emphasis of the importance of direct observation, and systematic gathering of data leading, step by step, to theory building. Van Gennep wanted social scientists to deal with living facts, rather than ‘dead’ and abstract social facts. Even a material object tells its own story, about the techniques behind its making, its use, and its social significance. Van Gennep was truly passionate about ethnographic details, real living details, artefacts, artistic techniques, paintings, beliefs, rites, production techniques, legends. He published widely on highly specialized topics, from weaving techniques to pottery making, and animal and property markings. 14 There was not a corner of the world that was not of interest to him. In her reconstruction, Belmont indicates that, prior to 1914, Arnold van Gennep was the only real ethnologist in France. Van Gennep wanted the social sciences to establish autonomy vis-à-vis history. This was an argument he carried into his establishment of folklore. In a programmatic piece from 1924 he stated with his usual clarity that ‘it is only gradually that we are healing from the disease of the nineteenth century, which we can call the historical mania, according to which all that is contemporary is significant only in so far as it has a bearing on the past, and which ultimately means that ... the Living are significant only in so far as they have a bearing on the Dead’ (as quoted and translated by Hafstein 1999). However, unlike so many other branches of the social sciences that wanted to liberate themselves from history and psychology, van Gennep was never simply closing doors. Far from only focusing on small-scale societies, he actually kept making systematic references to and comparisons with ‘historical societies’, like ancient Egypt, Rome and Greece. He was an expert on Homer. There was indeed nothing man-made that did not capture his ethnographic curiosity. It is within this horizon of a genuine life science that the concept of liminality belongs. We now need to make more explicit how this project differed from standard approaches in the social sciences. In other words, we need to engage with the controversy between Arnold van Gennep and Emile Durkheim. 14 In one of van Gennep’s short publications in Man (1909), he provides a single page response to a question raised by a woman who had found a highly peculiar netting technique among the Bushmen, asking the readers of Man whether similar techniques were to be found elsewhere.
